summary: DHH argues that business decisions are inherently uncertain and that even successful experiments don't produce universal truths. He warns against treating past wins or losses as definitive proof of what works, since most business experiments are imperfect and context-dependent. The post cautions that locking in conclusions from past bets—whether wins or losses—makes you a prisoner of flawed narratives. Instead, he advocates maintaining an open mind that treats all past results as provisional and subject to reinterpretation.
key_points: Business experiments rarely produce clean, definitive answers—confounding variables and second-order effects make perfect experiments nearly impossible
Winning a bet doesn't mean you've discovered truth; the conclusions drawn from success should remain suspect
The phrase 'we tried that, didn't work' is dangerously reductive—it forecloses future experimentation with different approaches
Good business people make calculated bets on incomplete information, but great ones avoid hardening those bets into rigid beliefs
Resisting the urge to write a definitive narrative keeps you open to reinterpretation and future opportunity
money_quotes: The fun bit about business is in all the answers you don't have.
The hallmark of a good business person is their ability to make calculated bets based on half a story and a hunch.
The key is to remember that what you won was a bet, not a trophy made of truth.
We tried SOMETHING and that SOMETHING didn't work. We could try SOMETHING ELSE and THAT might work.
If you don't, you'll eventually become a prisoner to every bet you've made.
It's better than okay to resist coming up with a definitive story either which way. Because as soon as you write that story down, it tends to become rigid rather than open to reinterpretation.
An open mind is always willing to let the story twist, willing to revisit the setup, and willing to test everything from timing to premise on another day.
key_insight: Business outcomes are bets won or lost, not truths discovered—hardening them into rigid narratives closes you off to future opportunities.
